rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE EDWARD G. GODOSHIAN REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST TRUST

rulings.Rulings: [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD Part 3 of 3] Sanctions are awarded under CCP sections 2017.020(b) and 2025.420(h) in the amount of $2,500 to Respondent and against Petitioner, which the Court finds to be a reasonable amount incurred in bringing this motion, including reasonable attorney's fees. Per the findings above, the motion was opposed without substantial justification. Sanctions are to be paid within 10 days of the receipt of the Court's order. Respondent shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on December 16, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. Respondent shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on December 16, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should enter their full names. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD End Part 3 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE EDWARD G. GODOSHIAN REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Respondent Linda K. Lester's Motion for Protective Order is granted in part and denied in part. The deposition of Respondent originally noticed for October 8, 2020, is ordered postponed to a mutually-agreed upon date after an order is entered on Respondent's Petition for Instructions Regarding Defense of Trust and Trustee at Trust Expense, currently set to be heard on February 2, 2021 (CCP section 2025.420(b)(3)). Production of the requested documents, subject to the court's protective order, will be due no later than one day prior to the deposition date. Generally, absent a claim of privilege or attorney work product, the party seeking discovery meets their initial burden in a discovery dispute by a fact-specific showing of relevance. (Kirkland v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 92, 98.) Once good cause is shown, the burden shifts to the opposing party to justify her objection. (Ibid.) Under CCP section 2017.020(a), "The court shall limit the scope of discovery if it determines that the burden, expense, or intrusiveness of that discovery clearly outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Respondent's motion for protective order is GRANTED as to Requests 7 and 8. Petitioner has not met the initial burden of a fact-specific showing of relevance for Requests 7 and 8 or that the Requests - encompassing decades of unfiltered documentation - is reasonably calculated to lead to admissible evidence. The requested production would lead to undue burden and expense, and unwarranted intrusion into Respondent's personal and financial records. The Court notes that Petitioner refused to narrow the requests to address the burden, expense or intrusiveness in the meet and confer documents presented with the motion. Respondent need not produce the documents specified in Request 7 and 8. (CCP sections 2025.420(b)(11) and 2031.060(b).) Respondent's motion for protective order is DENIED in part and GRANTED in part as to Requests 9 and 10. Petitioner met the initial burden of a fact-specific showing of relevance of Request 9 (statements regarding decedent's estate plan and Respondent's disinheritance), and Request 10 (documents from decedent's and Respondent's relationship). In response to these two Requests, Respondent already offered in meet and confer communications to produce non-privileged documents, limited to the relevant time period of the challenged trust and communications regarding decedent's estate planning documents. Respondent's proposed limitations to Requests 9 and 10 strike an appropriate balance. Production in response to Requests 9 and 10 is ordered limited to non-privileged communications regarding: Decedent's estate planning, the Trust, Respondent's alleged role as decedent's agent or representative, Respondent's alleged role as decedent's caregiver. The time period of production is ordered limited to January 1, 1998 through January 1, 2017. (CCP sections 2025.420(b)(11) and 2031.060(b).) [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE EDWARD G. GODOSHIAN REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD Part 2 of 3] Respondent's motion for protective order is GRANTED as to Requests 11 and 12. A discovery request over a privacy objection is not limitless. "[W]hen evidence sought to be discovered impacts on a person's constitutional right to privacy (Cal. Const., art. 1, section 1), limited protections come into play for that person. The protections extend both to a person's personal and financial matters. In ruling on discovery motions, the court must balance competing rights-the right of a litigant to discover relevant facts and the right of an individual to "maintain reasonable privacy." (Shaffer v. Superior Court (1995) 33 Cal. App. 4th 993, 999, citing (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court (1975) 15 Cal.3d 652, 656, emphasis in original.] Request 11 seeks "the computer that was used for making changes to" the Trust. Request 12 seeks "the computer files that were used for making changes to" the Trust. In the verified reply in support of the motion, Respondent avers that "the computer used to make changes to Decedent's trust did not belong to the Trustee, is not in the Trustee's possession or control, and, upon information and belief, was replaced, recycled or discarded." (Trustee's Reply, p. 7.) In litigating the motion, Petitioner appears to broaden Requests 11 and 12 to seek Respondent's personal computer. (See Petitioner's Opposition at p. 6 ["any computer used for Trust administration or for manipulating the contested trust instrument can be inspected'].) To the extent that the discovery seeks access to Respondent's personal computer, Petitioner does not meet the initial burden to make a fact-specific showing of relevance regarding an entire computer. The vague phrase "files that were used for making changes" to the Trust document is not a fact-specific showing of relevance, particularly in view of the verified response that Respondent does not possess the computer used to make changes to the trust. Petitioner does not make a sufficient showing of a right to discovery sufficient to counter-balance an individual's rights to maintain reasonable privacy in the personal and professional documents and files reasonably contained on a personal computer. Respondent need not produce the documents specified in Request 11 and 12. (CCP sections 2025.420(b)(11) and 2031.060(b).) Likewise, Request 13 for all of Respondents telephone records for the past decade does not meet the initial burden of fact-specific showing of relevance or reasonable calculation to lead to admissible evidence, or counterbalance Respondent's privacy interest in who she has called and who has called her over the past decade. The Court notes that Petitioner refused to narrow the requests to address the burden, expense or intrusiveness in the meet and confer documents presented with the motion. Respondent need not produce the documents specified in Request 13. (CCP sections 2025.420(b)(11) and 2031.060(b).) Respondent's motion for protective order is DENIED in part and GRANTED in part as to Request 14. Some portion of the material sought in Request 14 may be relevant or lead to discovery of relevant evidence, but the request as framed fails to make a fact specific showing of relevance. Respondent's offer in the meet and confer materials to search for and produce specific categories of documents strikes an appropriate balance. Respondent's response to Request 14 may be limited to correspondence with decedent related to decedent's trusts, will, estate plan, and testamentary wishes. If Respondent is withholding documents based on privilege, she is ordered to produce a privilege log, as applicable. [CORRECTED TENTATIVE, CORRECTED AS TO SCRIVENER'S ERROR AS TO SANCTIONS AWARD Part 2 of 3]
